title: Process Life Cycle — 5-State Model
examTip: Five states: New → Ready → Running → Waiting → Terminated. Only ONE process can be in Running state at a time per CPU. The Dispatcher moves process from Ready to Running. Time-quantum expiry sends Running → Ready (preemption).
theory:
The Process Life Cycle (also called Process State Model) defines all the states a process can be in during its lifetime in an operating system, and the valid transitions between those states.

New State: A process enters the New state when it is first created by the OS (via fork() system call in Unix/Linux or CreateProcess() in Windows). The OS allocates a Process Control Block (PCB) and assigns a Process ID (PID).

Ready State: The process is loaded into main memory and is waiting in the Ready Queue for CPU time. All conditions for execution are met except CPU availability. Multiple processes can be in the Ready state simultaneously.

Running State: The process is actively executing on the CPU. On a single-core processor, only ONE process can be in Running state at any given time. The CPU executes instructions from this process.

Waiting (Blocked) State: The process cannot proceed until an external event occurs — typically an I/O operation (disk read/write, keyboard input, network packet) or a synchronization event (mutex, semaphore). The process relinquishes the CPU voluntarily and moves to the I/O Wait Queue.

Terminated State: The process has finished execution or has been killed. The OS deallocates all resources, closes file handles, removes the PCB, and informs the parent process.

Key Transitions: Admitted (New→Ready) occurs when memory is allocated. The Dispatcher (part of the CPU Scheduler) performs the Ready→Running transition through a context switch. Time Quantum Expiry (Running→Ready) is preemption in time-sharing systems like Round Robin scheduling. I/O Request (Running→Waiting) is a voluntary relinquishment of the CPU.

Context Switching: When transitioning between states, the OS saves the process state (registers, PC, stack pointer) into its PCB and restores the state of the next process to run.
keyPoints: Five states: New, Ready, Running, Waiting (Blocked), Terminated
Only ONE process can be in Running state per CPU at any time
The Dispatcher performs the Ready→Running transition via a context switch
I/O request: Running→Waiting (voluntary); Time quantum expiry: Running→Ready (preemption)
Process Control Block (PCB) stores all process information: PID, state, PC, registers, memory map
useCases: CPU scheduling algorithms: FCFS, SJF, Round Robin, Priority Scheduling all manage state transitions
Multiprogramming OS: keeping CPU busy by switching between Ready processes during I/O waits
Real-time OS: tracking process states with strict timing constraints for embedded systems
